text: My name is Daniel Murariu of Allegheny General Hospital and I'd like to describe a novel robotically harvested vertical rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap following robotic abdominal perineal resection. I'd like to thank Dr. Richard Fortunato for the colorectal portion. We have a fifty-two year old male with BMI of thirty-three with a distal locally advanced rectal cancer who had undergone a neoadjuvant with chemoradiation. This marks the skin paddle and the proposed X is the Doppler bowl vessel. The patient underwent an extrasynthetic perineal resection due to the size and location of the tumor.

The surgeon places two additional ports on the left side to triangulate the right rectus abdominis muscle. An assistant palpates the abdomen while the surgeon marks the superior aspect of the dissection with electrocautery. The posterior rectus sheath is incised to expose the rectus abdominis muscle, which is dissected out superiorly and inferiorly using robotic assistance. The muscle flap is also dissected laterally, aided by insufflation and enhanced visualization.

The surgeon harvests and dissects the pedicle of the deep inferior epigastric vessels, facilitating the rotation of the muscle flap into the pelvic defect. The rectus abdominis muscle is cut at its origin at the pubic crest using a ligature device, marking the completion of the intra-abdominal elevation component.

The surgeon incises the skin paddle and elevates the muscle superiorly under direct visualization. Indocyanine Green is injected, demonstrating good perfusion of the skin paddle, which is then placed into the abdominal cavity and passed into the pelvic defect, where it is sutured into position.
